questions.question: Define CPU execution time and list the three factors it depends on according to the CPU performance equation.
keyPoints: CPU execution time is the total time a CPU spends computing a specific task.
It depends on the Instruction Count (number of instructions executed).
It depends on the Cycles Per Instruction (CPI), the average number of clock cycles per instruction.
It depends on the Clock Cycle Time (duration of one clock cycle).

questions.question: Explain the concept of Amdahl's Law and how it is used to calculate the overall speedup of a system when improving only a portion of it. Use an example to illustrate your point.
keyPoints: Amdahl's Law states that the overall speedup is limited by the fraction of the task that is improved.
The law is defined by the formula: Speedup_overall = 1 / [(1 - Fraction_enhanced) + (Fraction_enhanced / Speedup_enhanced)]
For example, if 40% of a program (Fraction_enhanced=0.4) is sped up by a factor of 5 (Speedup_enhanced=5), the overall speedup is 1 / [(1-0.4) + (0.4/5)] = 1 / [0.6 + 0.08] = ~1.47.
It demonstrates that even a large improvement to one part of a system has a diminishing return if that part is not a significant fraction of the whole.

questions.question: Compare and contrast the two primary metrics for measuring processor performance, CPU execution time and MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second). Discuss the advantages and significant pitfalls of using MIPS as a performance benchmark.
keyPoints: CPU execution time is a direct and reliable measure of performance because it measures the actual task completion time.
MIPS is an indirect measure calculated as (Instruction Count / (Execution Time * 10^6)).
A key pitfall of MIPS is that it favors processors that execute more instructions per cycle, even if those instructions are not necessarily contributing to the completion of the task at hand.
MIPS can be misleading because it does not account for the complexity or usefulness of the instructions being executed.
CPU execution time, on the other hand, directly measures the time it takes to complete a task, making it a more accurate and reliable metric for performance comparison.
